location.origin: Seattle, Washington. Poor Moon was a Seattle-based indie folk/rock band. Duwamish ancestral territory. The band emerged from the Fleet Foxes orbit -- Christian Wargo and Casey Wescott forming a side project while remaining Fleet Foxes members.
location.formative: Seattle, Washington. Poor Moon's formative geography is Seattle's indie folk ecosystem -- the same scene that produced Fleet Foxes, the same studios, the same venues, the same Sub Pop label. The side project inherits the parent act's geography entirely.
location.career: Poor Moon: Poor Moon (2012, Sub Pop Records). One album. Dreamy, reverb-heavy indie folk/rock -- spacier and more psychedelic than Fleet Foxes. Side project of Fleet Foxes members Christian Wargo and Casey Wescott. Sub Pop Records (Seattle) -- second Sub Pop artist in the engine after Sam Beam (68). One-album band -- second defunct/ended artist after Pickwick (62).

labels: Sub Pop Records (Seattle, WA -- founded 1986 by Bruce Pavitt and Jonathan Poneman; second Sub Pop artist in the engine after Sam Beam at degree 68; the label that defined Seattle from grunge through indie folk; Fleet Foxes' label; the gravitational center of Seattle's indie ecosystem; Poor Moon 2012)

members.name: Christian Wargo
members.note: Also of Fleet Foxes (degree 53) and Crystal Skulls. Multi-band musician -- a node in Seattle's interconnected indie ecosystem. Wargo links degree 74 to degree 53 through shared personnel. In Fleet Foxes: part of the communal harmony. In Poor Moon: part of the reverb dream. Same voice, different architecture.

members.name: Casey Wescott
members.note: Also of Fleet Foxes (degree 53). Multi-instrumentalist whose arrangements in Fleet Foxes are dense and harmonic. In Poor Moon, his contributions are more exposed -- individual instrument voices audible in the reverb wash rather than subsumed into the collective harmony.

associated_musicians.name: Fleet Foxes
associated_musicians.note: The strongest inter-degree connection in the engine: shared personnel. Christian Wargo and Casey Wescott are members of both Fleet Foxes and Poor Moon. Fleet Foxes' communal harmony (53) is the parent aesthetic. Poor Moon's reverb wash (74) is the offspring. Same musicians, different architecture. The choir becomes the echo.

associated_musicians.name: Sam Beam (Iron & Wine)
associated_musicians.note: Both Sub Pop artists. Beam's Sub Pop connection was institutional -- the visiting artist (Southern musician on Seattle label). Poor Moon's Sub Pop connection is organic -- a Seattle band on Seattle's label. Both on Sub Pop. Different relationships to place.

associated_musicians.name: Pickwick
associated_musicians.note: Both defunct/ended artists. Both left brief catalogs. Pickwick was an independent band that ran its course. Poor Moon was a side project whose members returned to Fleet Foxes. Both finite. Different causes of finitude.

associated_musicians.name: Phil Elverum
associated_musicians.note: Both atmospheric. Elverum's atmosphere is lo-fi -- tape hiss, analog reduction. Poor Moon's atmosphere is reverb-heavy -- lush, spacious. Both build sonic environments. Lo-fi reduces. Reverb expands. Different technologies of atmosphere.

associated_musicians.name: Chris Walla
associated_musicians.note: Both relate to band membership patterns. Walla departed Death Cab permanently -- the departed member. Poor Moon's members maintained dual membership in Fleet Foxes and Poor Moon -- the side project. Departure vs. straddling. Severance vs. simultaneity.

theory_nodes.concept: The reverb wash (seventy-fifth acoustic element -- the dreamy, reverb-heavy atmospheric quality where instruments and voices dissolve into spacious, psychedelic haze; reverb as environment rather than effect; sound dispersing into atmospheric space; the dream state made sonic; first reverb-defined artist in the engine)
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- reverb-heavy indie folk/rock on Poor Moon (2012, Sub Pop). The reverb is not an effect applied to the sound but the environment the sound inhabits. Every element passes through reverb processing that creates the illusion of vast space.

Contrast with the layered loop (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- the layered loop multiplies a single voice through technology, building density. The reverb wash dissolves sounds into atmosphere, creating the illusion of infinite space. The layered loop accumulates. The reverb wash disperses.

Contrast with the communal harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- particularly significant given shared personnel. The communal harmony is built by many voices in precise harmonic alignment. The reverb wash is built by electronic processing. Composition versus production. Harmony versus atmosphere. The choir versus the echo.

Contrast with the hush (degree 68, Beam) -- the hush is silence and quietness. The reverb wash is full, atmospheric, enveloping. Both intimate. The hush is intimate through proximity. The reverb wash is intimate through dissolution.
The reverb wash operates at E=3 -- middle energy. The reverb creates a sense of vastness without volume. The music drifts rather than drives. Floating energy. The dream state.
First reverb-defined artist in the engine. Technology as environment: the reverb pedal transforms the recording space from a physical location into an infinite acoustic room.

Fleet Foxes connection: same musicians (Wargo, Wescott), different acoustic elements. The communal harmony (53) is a human phenomenon -- voices singing together. The reverb wash (74) is a technological phenomenon -- electronic processing creating spatial illusion.
theory_nodes.description: The reverb wash is the seventy-fifth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the dreamy, reverb-heavy atmospheric quality where instruments and voices dissolve into spacious, psychedelic haze. In Poor Moon's music, reverb is not an effect but an environment -- sound dispersing into atmospheric space. First reverb-defined artist in the engine.

ancestors: The layered loop (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- both use technology as instrument. The layered loop accumulates; the reverb wash disperses.
The communal harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- same musicians, different acoustic elements. Harmony versus atmosphere.
The hush (degree 68, Beam) -- both intimate. Proximity versus dissolution.
genealogy.relationship: Seventy-five acoustic elements across seventy-five degrees. The reverb wash follows the layered loop -- from vocal accumulation to atmospheric dispersion. Track element 76 at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: The side project (seventy-fifth artist-city pattern -- band members from a bigger act creating music under a different name; dual membership; the creative space between albums of the primary act; the same musicians exploring different aesthetic registers; Fleet Foxes members forming Poor Moon)
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- Christian Wargo and Casey Wescott of Fleet Foxes forming a side project. Dreamy, reverb-heavy indie folk/rock distinct from Fleet Foxes' baroque folk harmonies. Same musicians, different architecture.

Contrast with the departed member (degree 67, Chris Walla) -- the departed member leaves the primary band permanently. The side project maintains dual membership. Walla departed Death Cab. Wargo and Wescott remained in Fleet Foxes while forming Poor Moon.
Contrast with the solo artist (degrees 68, 71, 72) -- the solo artist is an individual under their own name. The side project is a group under a band name, distinct from the primary act.

The side project exists in the gap between the primary act's albums. Poor Moon formed between Fleet Foxes' Helplessness Blues (2011) and Crack-Up (2017). The side project fills silence with different music.
The side project as circulation: musicians in Seattle flow between projects, carrying influences across band boundaries. Christian Wargo alone connects Fleet Foxes, Poor Moon, and Crystal Skulls.
First side project in the engine. Opens a pattern for tracking how musicians circulate between multiple acts.
theory_nodes.description: The side project is the seventy-fifth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes band members from a bigger act creating music under a different name while maintaining dual membership. Poor Moon is a Fleet Foxes side project. Same musicians, different architecture. The creative space between albums.

ancestors: The departed member (degree 67, Chris Walla) -- leaves permanently. The side project maintains dual membership.
The international student (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- trains abroad and returns. The side project forms locally from existing relationships.
The solo artist (degrees 68, 71, 72) -- individual vision. The side project is collaborative alternative vision.
genealogy.relationship: Seventy-five artist-city patterns across seventy-five degrees. The side project follows the international student -- from global circulation to local reconfiguration. Track pattern 76 at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the reverb trail (seventy-fourth deployment; the blue note that appears in the decay of reverberant sound; the shimmer between direct signal and spatial reflections; pitch blurring at its edges as it bounces through reverberant space; the blue note of spatial dissolution)
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- reverb-heavy production on Poor Moon (2012, Sub Pop). When a note is sung or played, the direct sound arrives first, followed by a cascade of reflections. In the space where direct sound and reverberant reflections coexist, the pitches are almost but not quite identical, creating a shimmer.

Contrast with the echo chamber (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- the echo chamber is the blue note between a voice and its looped duplicate, a temporal blue note. The reverb trail is the blue note between a sound and its spatial reflections. Temporal versus spatial.

Contrast with the thread (degree 70, Horse Feathers) -- the thread is between two different instruments approaching the same pitch. The reverb trail is between one sound and its own reflections. Two sources versus one source heard multiple times.

The reverb trail as blue note: the reverb creates a constant shimmer -- a haze of almost-consonant reflections that blur the boundaries between notes. This shimmer is the reverb trail: the pitch that was clear at its source becoming ambiguous as it bounces through reverberant space.
The reverb trail is ubiquitous in Poor Moon's music. Every vocal line, every guitar chord, every instrumental element carries a reverberant tail where the original pitch shimmers and blurs.
theory_nodes.description: The reverb trail is the seventy-fourth blue note deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the blue note that appears in the decay of reverberant sound -- the shimmer between direct signal and spatial reflections, the original pitch blurring at its edges as it bounces through reverberant space. In Poor Moon's music, the reverb creates a constant shimmer where clarity dissolves into atmospheric haze.

ancestors: Blue note -- the echo chamber (degree 72, Briana Marela): temporal displacement between a voice and its loop. The reverb trail is spatial displacement between a sound and its reflections.
Blue note -- the thread (degree 70, Horse Feathers): two instruments approaching the same pitch. The reverb trail is one sound and its own reflections.
All seventy-three prior blue note deployments (degrees 0-73)
genealogy.relationship: Seventy-four deployments across seventy-five degrees. Track deployment 75 at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: Indie Folk/Rock at Genre Stage 56 -- hybrid genre acknowledging both folk melodic sensibility and rock electric textures; the porous boundary between folk and rock in Seattle's indie scene; the side project's genre as distinct from the parent act's pure indie folk
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- Indie Folk/Rock. Genre Stage 56.

Genre progression: the hybrid classification reflects Poor Moon's position between folk (acoustic guitars, pastoral melody) and rock (electric guitars, reverb, rhythmic drive). The /Rock suffix marks a return of electric energy.
Contrast with Fleet Foxes (degree 53) -- pure Indie Folk. Poor Moon's hybrid genre is the side project's aesthetic distinction from its parent act.
Contrast with Art Pop (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- Art Pop is conceptual and sonic-experimental. Indie Folk/Rock is genre-boundary and atmosphere-driven.
The hybrid genre reflects Seattle's porous genre boundaries: the same musicians play in folk bands and rock bands, the same instruments appear in different configurations.
theory_nodes.description: Indie Folk/Rock at degree 74 establishes Genre Stage 56. A hybrid genre acknowledging both the folk melodic sensibility and the rock electric textures in Poor Moon's music. The /Rock suffix marks a return of electric instrumentation. The hybrid classification reflects the porous boundary between folk and rock in Seattle's indie scene.

ancestors: Art Pop at degree 72 (Briana Marela) -- Genre Stage 54. Indie Folk/Rock at 74 advances to Stage 56.
Indie Folk at degree 53 (Fleet Foxes) -- the parent act's genre. The side project's genre hybrid adds /Rock.
All prior genre stages in the engine.
genealogy.relationship: Genre Stage 56 at degree 74. Indie Folk/Rock hybrid. Track genre at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: E=3 at degree 74 -- nineteenth E=3 deployment; distribution 3:25:19:18:10:2; E=3 extends lead over E=4 (19 vs 18); floating energy -- reverb-heavy dream state; three of last five degrees at E=3
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- E=3. Middle energy. Dreamy, reverb-heavy music that drifts rather than drives. Floating energy. The dream state.
E=3 rises to 19 deployments. Distribution after degree 74: E1:3, E2:25, E3:19, E4:18, E5:10, E6:2. Total: 75.
E=3 extends lead over E=4 (19 vs 18). Three of last five degrees at E=3 (Horse Feathers 70, Briana Marela 72, Poor Moon 74).
E=3 as reverb energy: the reverb creates a sense of vastness without volume. The music fills space through dispersion rather than amplification. Present but unhurried. Dense without being forceful.
E=3 cluster: the engine is in a middle-energy run reflecting the folk/indie/atmospheric genre quadrant.
theory_nodes.description: E=3 at degree 74 is the nineteenth E=3 deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. Energy distribution: E1:3, E2:25, E3:19, E4:18, E5:10, E6:2. E=3 extends its lead over E=4 (19 vs 18). Three of the last five degrees at E=3. The E=3 at degree 74 reflects the dreamy, reverb-heavy floating energy.

ancestors: E=3 at degree 72 (Briana Marela): vocal looping energy. The reverb energy at 74 is similar -- atmospheric density without volume.
E=3 at degree 70 (Horse Feathers): chamber ensemble energy.
E=3 at previous deployments: 18th before degree 74. E=3 extends lead at 74.
genealogy.relationship: Nineteen E=3 deployments across seventy-five degrees. Distribution 3:25:19:18:10:2. E=3 leads E=4. Track at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: Sub Pop Returns at degree 74 -- second Sub Pop artist; first was Sam Beam (68); Seattle's defining label hosts Fleet Foxes side project; Sub Pop as recurring institution; local versus national indie labels
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- second Sub Pop artist in the engine. Poor Moon (2012, Sub Pop).
First Sub Pop: Sam Beam (degree 68) -- the visiting artist, institutional connection. Second Sub Pop: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- organic connection, Seattle band on Seattle's label.
Sub Pop becomes a recurring institution. Two artists across six degrees. The label's roster is so deep that additional Sub Pop entries are inevitable.
Contrast with Jagjaguwar (degree 72, Briana Marela) -- Jagjaguwar is national indie (Indianapolis). Sub Pop is local indie (Seattle). Both independent. Different geographies of independence.
Fleet Foxes connection: Fleet Foxes are Sub Pop artists. Poor Moon (Fleet Foxes side project) is also on Sub Pop. The label holds both the parent and offspring acts.
theory_nodes.description: Sub Pop Records returns at degree 74 as the second Sub Pop artist in the engine. First was Sam Beam at degree 68. Sub Pop becomes a recurring institution. Seattle's defining label hosts a Fleet Foxes side project. Contrast with Jagjaguwar (72, national) -- Sub Pop is the local gravitational center.

ancestors: Sub Pop at degree 68 (Sam Beam): first Sub Pop artist. Institutional connection.
Jagjaguwar at degree 72 (Briana Marela): national indie. Sub Pop is local indie.
Kill Rock Stars at degree 70 (Horse Feathers): political indie, Olympia. Sub Pop is commercial tastemaker, Seattle.
genealogy.relationship: Sub Pop returns at degree 74. Second Sub Pop artist. Track label at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: Gender: male-led at degree 74 -- ratio 21:54 (~28.0%); dips from degree 73; 30% threshold unmet; two consecutive male-led degrees
examples: Poor Moon (degree 74) -- male-led band. Gender ratio: 21:54 (~28.0%).
The ratio dips slightly from degree 73. The 30% threshold remains unmet.
Two consecutive male-led degrees. The engine has been approaching 30% since degree 66 (Neko Case) but has not crossed.
theory_nodes.description: Gender at degree 74: Poor Moon is male-led. Ratio: 21:54 (~28.0%). The 30% threshold remains unmet. Two consecutive male-led degrees.

ancestors: Male at degree 73: ratio after 73. Dips slightly at 74.
genealogy.relationship: Gender ratio 21:54 (~28.0%) at degree 74. 30% threshold approaching. Track at degree 75.

theory_nodes.concept: Territorial acknowledgment: Duwamish territory at degree 74 -- Seattle-based band; Duwamish territory continuous; seven territories mapped
examples: Degree 74 -- Duwamish territory (Seattle). Poor Moon is Seattle-based. The Duwamish territorial acknowledgment continues from multiple prior Seattle degrees.

Seven Coast Salish territories remain mapped: Duwamish (Seattle), Multnomah (Portland, 57), Lummi/Nooksack (Bellingham, 58), Snohomish (Everett, 63), Puyallup (Tacoma, 66), Squaxin Island/Nisqually (Olympia, 70).
theory_nodes.description: The territorial acknowledgment at degree 74 is Duwamish territory -- Poor Moon is Seattle-based. Seven Coast Salish territories remain mapped.

ancestors: Duwamish territory at multiple prior Seattle degrees.
Seven territories mapped through degree 74.
genealogy.relationship: Duwamish territory at degree 74. Seven territories mapped. Track at degree 75.

cross_references.relationship: Band members shared -- the strongest possible inter-degree connection. Christian Wargo and Casey Wescott are members of both Fleet Foxes and Poor Moon. Fleet Foxes' communal harmony (53) is the parent aesthetic. Poor Moon's reverb wash (74) is the offspring. Same musicians, different architecture. The choir becomes the echo.

Sub Pop shared -- second Sub Pop artist in the engine. Beam's connection was institutional (visiting artist). Poor Moon's connection is organic (Seattle band on Seattle's label). Both Sub Pop. Different relationships to place.

Both defunct/ended artists. Both left brief catalogs. Pickwick was independent. Poor Moon was a side project. Both finite. Different causes of finitude -- choice versus gravitational return to primary act.
Both atmospheric. Elverum's atmosphere is lo-fi -- tape hiss, analog reduction. Poor Moon's atmosphere is reverb-heavy -- lush, spacious. Both build sonic environments. Lo-fi reduces. Reverb expands.
Band membership patterns. Walla departed Death Cab permanently -- the departed member. Poor Moon's members maintained dual membership -- the side project. Departure versus straddling.
